class_schedule.topic: What Is Educational Technology? Definitions and Scope
learning_objectives: Define educational technology as a field of study and practice
Distinguish between technology in education and technology of education
Trace the historical evolution of educational technology from film to AI
readings: Educational Technology: A Definition with Commentary (AECT, 2008) Ch. 1
Reiser, R. - A History of Instructional Design and Technology

lecture_notes.introduction: Educational technology is not just about gadgets in classrooms. The field encompasses the systematic design, development, and evaluation of processes and resources for learning. This session establishes the scope of what you will study.
key_concepts: AECT definition: facilitating learning and improving performance
Technology in education vs. technology of education
Historical arc: film, radio, television, CAI, internet, mobile, AI
The SAMR model: Substitution, Augmentation, Modification, Redefinition
TPACK framework: Technological, Pedagogical, and Content Knowledge
lecture_notes.demonstration: SAMR classification exercise: categorize 10 examples of classroom technology use along the SAMR continuum

review.value: Four levels of technology integration: Substitution (direct replacement), Augmentation (functional improvement), Modification (task redesign), Redefinition (creating new tasks previously inconceivable)
Effective technology integration requires the intersection of technological knowledge, pedagogical knowledge, and content knowledge

class_schedule.topic: Digital Literacy and the 21st Century Learner
learning_objectives: Define digital literacy and its components
Evaluate claims about digital natives and generational differences
Identify the skills learners need to thrive in technology-rich environments
readings: Prensky, M. - Digital Natives, Digital Immigrants (with critical response)

lecture_notes.introduction: The claim that young people are 'digital natives' who naturally understand technology is widespread but empirically unsupported. This session examines what digital literacy actually means and why it must be taught, not assumed.
key_concepts: Components of digital literacy: information, media, ICT literacy
Critique of the digital native myth
Digital divide: access, skills, and usage gaps
Critical evaluation of online information
Digital citizenship and online safety
lecture_notes.demonstration: Lateral reading exercise: evaluate three online sources using fact-checking strategies developed by the Stanford History Education Group
setup_requirements: Pre-selected sources of varying reliability for evaluation

review.value: Goes beyond access to include differences in digital skills, the quality of use, and the ability to leverage technology for learning and economic advancement
Research shows young people's technology skills are uneven; familiarity with social media does not equal information literacy or productive learning use

learning_objectives: Categorize educational technology tools by function and pedagogical purpose
Evaluate technology tools using research-based selection criteria
Explain the one-to-one computing model and its research outcomes

lecture_notes.introduction: With thousands of educational technology products available, educators need frameworks for evaluating and selecting tools that genuinely support learning rather than adding complexity without benefit.
key_concepts: Categories: productivity, communication, assessment, simulation, creation
One-to-one computing programs: implementation and evidence
BYOD policies: benefits and challenges
Evaluation criteria: alignment, usability, accessibility, evidence base, cost
The ISTE Standards for Students as a selection framework
lecture_notes.demonstration: Tool evaluation exercise: use a rubric to rate three competing educational apps for the same learning objective
setup_requirements: Access to three sample educational apps or tools

review.value: Ask: What is the pedagogical purpose? Is there research evidence? Does it meet accessibility standards? What is the total cost of ownership?
Meta-analyses show modest positive effects when accompanied by professional development, curricular integration, and clear learning goals

learning_objectives: Explain Mayer's cognitive theory of multimedia learning
Apply multimedia learning principles to evaluate and create educational materials
Identify common violations of multimedia principles in existing content
readings: Clark, R. & Mayer, R. - e-Learning and the Science of Instruction (excerpts)

lecture_notes.introduction: Not all multimedia is created equal. Richard Mayer's research program has identified specific principles that determine whether multimedia helps or hinders learning. These principles are the foundation of evidence-based instructional media design.
key_concepts: Dual channel assumption: visual and auditory processing
Limited capacity assumption: working memory constraints
Active processing assumption: selecting, organizing, integrating
Key principles: coherence, signaling, redundancy, spatial contiguity, temporal contiguity
Modality principle: narration over on-screen text for complex visuals
lecture_notes.demonstration: Critique and redesign: take a poorly designed slide deck and apply multimedia principles to improve it

review.value: People learn better when extraneous material is excluded; interesting but irrelevant content hurts learning
People learn better from animation and narration than from animation, narration, and on-screen text simultaneously

learning_objectives: Describe the evolution from Web 1.0 to Web 3.0 in educational contexts
Evaluate the affordances and constraints of web-based learning
Analyze the role of open educational resources (OER)

lecture_notes.introduction: The internet has transformed education from a place-bound activity to one that can happen anywhere, anytime. But this transformation brings new challenges alongside new opportunities.
key_concepts: Web 1.0 (consumption), Web 2.0 (participation), Web 3.0 (semantic/AI)
MOOCs: promise, reality, and completion rates
Open Educational Resources and Creative Commons licensing
Social media as informal learning environments
Privacy and data concerns in web-based learning
lecture_notes.demonstration: OER treasure hunt: find, evaluate, and remix an OER resource for a specific learning objective using OER Commons
setup_requirements: Access to OER Commons and Creative Commons license guide

review.value: Open Educational Resources are freely accessible, openly licensed materials that can be used, adapted, and redistributed; they reduce cost barriers but raise sustainability questions
Average completion rates of 5-15% challenge the democratization narrative; successful learners tend to already be educated and motivated

class_schedule.topic: Emerging Technologies: AI, VR, and What Comes Next
learning_objectives: Identify emerging technologies with potential educational applications
Evaluate emerging technology claims using the Gartner Hype Cycle
Develop a critical framework for assessing new educational technologies

lecture_notes.introduction: New technologies constantly promise to revolutionize education. This final session equips you with frameworks for evaluating these claims critically, so you can distinguish genuine innovation from hype.
key_concepts: Gartner Hype Cycle: trigger, peak, trough, slope, plateau
AI in education: intelligent tutoring, adaptive learning, automated assessment
VR/AR: immersive learning environments and their evidence base
Blockchain and credentialing
Critical evaluation framework: Who benefits? What is the evidence? What are the costs?
lecture_notes.demonstration: Technology futures exercise: place five current educational technologies on the Hype Cycle and defend your placement with evidence
setup_requirements: Research summaries for five selected technologies

review.value: Technologies pass through predictable phases of overenthusiasm and disillusionment before reaching productive use; educators should look for slope of enlightenment technologies
For any new technology, ask: What problem does it solve? What is the evidence of effectiveness? Who benefits and who is excluded? What are the implementation costs?

quizzes.topic: Educational Technology Foundations and Digital Literacy

questions.question: Using the SAMR model, classify the following scenario and explain your reasoning: A teacher replaces paper worksheets with identical digital worksheets that students complete on tablets.
questions.answer: This is Substitution, the lowest level of the SAMR model. The technology directly replaces the paper worksheet with no functional change. The task, cognitive demand, and student experience remain essentially identical. For this to reach Augmentation, the digital worksheet would need to add functionality (e.g., immediate feedback, embedded links to resources). For Modification or Redefinition, the task itself would need to change.

rubric.key: Explains why no functional change occurs
Describes what higher SAMR levels would require

questions.question: Why do researchers consider the 'digital native' concept problematic? Provide two evidence-based reasons.
questions.answer: First, empirical studies show that young people's technology skills are highly variable and do not correlate reliably with age or generation. Students may be fluent in social media but unable to evaluate search results or use productivity tools. Second, the concept can lead educators to assume students do not need digital literacy instruction, resulting in a failure to teach critical skills like information evaluation, privacy management, and productive technology use for learning.

rubric.key: Connects to implications for educational practice

questions.question: A training video features a narrator reading bullet points that appear on screen while an animation plays in the background. Which multimedia learning principles are being violated? Explain the impact on learning.
questions.answer: This violates the Redundancy Principle (presenting identical information in both narration and on-screen text overloads the visual channel) and the Coherence Principle (the background animation is likely extraneous, competing for visual attention). The combined effect splits the learner's visual attention between text, animation, and narration processing, increasing cognitive load and reducing the mental resources available for understanding the content.

rubric.key: Explains cognitive load impact on learning

questions.question: What are Open Educational Resources (OER)? Name one benefit and one challenge of OER adoption in higher education.
questions.answer: OER are teaching, learning, and research materials that are freely available and openly licensed (typically Creative Commons), allowing use, adaptation, and redistribution. Benefit: They significantly reduce textbook costs for students, improving access and equity (studies show students save hundreds of dollars per year and course outcomes are maintained or improved). Challenge: Sustainability — creating and maintaining high-quality OER requires ongoing investment of time and resources, and there is no established business model to fund continuous updates and quality assurance.

major_assignments.title: Educational Technology Audit and Recommendation Report
major_assignments.description: Conduct an audit of technology use in a real educational setting (a school, training program, or online course you have access to). Document what technologies are being used, classify them using SAMR, evaluate them against multimedia learning principles, and produce a professional recommendation report with evidence-based suggestions for improvement.
objectives: Apply SAMR and TPACK frameworks to evaluate real technology integration
Use multimedia learning principles as evaluation criteria
Produce a professional recommendation document suitable for a real audience
deliverables: Technology inventory table with SAMR classifications

criteria: Comprehensive inventory of technologies in the setting
TPACK analysis of teacher/trainer technology integration
Applies multimedia learning principles to evaluate materials
Identifies both effective uses and missed opportunities
Recommendations are specific, actionable, and prioritized
Each recommendation is supported by research evidence
Implementation considerations (cost, training, timeline) are addressed
Report is suitable for a professional audience
Clear writing, logical organization, proper formatting
Thorough technology audit with accurate framework application
Insightful evaluation grounded in learning science

quizzes.description: Bi-weekly quizzes on educational technology concepts and frameworks

participation.description: In-class activities, tool evaluations, and discussion contributions

midterm_examination.description: Covers definitions, history, frameworks, digital literacy, and multimedia principles

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam with emphasis on evaluation and application of educational technology concepts

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. More than 3 unexcused absences will reduce the final grade by a half-letter. Lab activities cannot be made up.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work is penalized 5% per day for up to 5 days. Extensions require 48-hour advance notice and documented justification.
course_policies.collaboration: Collaborative discussion is encouraged. All submitted work must be individually authored. Proper APA citation is required for all sources.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Plagiarism, fabrication, or submitting work done by others (including AI-generated text without disclosure) results in a zero and referral to the academic integrity office.
course_policies.technology_requirements: A computer with reliable internet access. Ability to install and test educational software and apps. Access to a real educational setting for the audit assignment.
